cards.text: To time code in C++, use the {{c1::<chrono>}} library from C++11, which provides high-resolution timing functionality.
To get the current time point, use {{c1::std::chrono::steady_clock::now()}}.

cards.question: What does this timing code do?
cards.answer: Measures how long sortArray() takes to execute, storing the elapsed time in seconds as a double.
cards.explanation: It captures the time before and after the function call, then calculates the difference (duration) between them.

cards.front: Why use std::chrono::steady_clock instead of std::chrono::system_clock?
cards.back: steady_clock is monotonic (never goes backwards), making it reliable for measuring intervals. system_clock can be adjusted (e.g., daylight savings, NTP), causing inaccurate measurements.

cards.text: To convert a duration to milliseconds, use {{c1::std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(elapsed).count()}}.

cards.explanation:
Timing can be affected by:
  • Compiler optimizations (may eliminate dead code)
  • System load (other processes competing)
  • CPU frequency scaling
  • Caching effects (first run vs subsequent runs)
  • Small variations between runs
Run multiple iterations and average results.

cards.text: std::chrono::duration<{{c1::double}}> stores elapsed time as a floating-point value in {{c2::seconds}} by default.

cards.front: How do you get the numeric value from a std::chrono::duration?
cards.back: Call the .count() member function, which returns the number of ticks in the duration's underlying representation.

cards.question: What's wrong with this timing code?
cards.answer: Compiler may optimize away expensiveCalc() since result is unused, giving inaccurate timing.
cards.explanation: To prevent this, ensure the result is used in a way the compiler can't optimize away (e.g., print it, or mark it as volatile).

cards.back: A clock that only moves forward, never backwards (even if system time is adjusted). Example: std::chrono::steady_clock

cards.front: What is the typical use case for timing code with std::chrono?
cards.back: Comparing performance of different implementations or algorithms to determine which is faster in practice.
